Welcome to the replication/reproduction team! You can get started with your preregistration by clicking here. CONFIDENTIALITY DISCLAIMER: Other teams are making predictions about the outcomes of many different studies, not knowing which studies have been selected for replication. As a consequence, the success of this project requires full confidentiality of the research process, including peer review. This includes privacy about which studies have been selected for replication and all aspects of the discussion about these replication designs. Table of contents Table of contents Review Information

Instructions for Preregistration Reviewers Your role is to review preregistered research designs for clarity, completeness, and quality. For the purposes of a SCORE project, a preregistration is high quality if it generates a protocol that is a good faith attempt to replicate or reproduce the original finding. In sum, focus on whether differences in original versus replication/reproduction protocols are substantively anticipated to matter for claims in the original paper (and the broader field), and be biased against spending time on speculative concerns that do not have an evidence base. Please start by reading the Existing Data Reviewer Criteria checklist. For each section, you should evaluate whether the description is complete, whether deviations from the original study (or additions, if the information was not available) are documented, and whether, all told, the decisions are consistent with a good faith replication or reproduction. You don’t need to fill out a copy of the checklist, but should use it as a guide to the types of information that should be present in a finished preregistration - remember that not all items will apply to all projects. At the very end of the pregistration is a final reviewer checklist where you can give your evaluation of the preregistration as a whole. When you are completely finished reviewing, please comment ‘DONE’ on your name on the first page of this document. These projects are intended to be robust, high quality studies, and in some cases, this will involve deviation from the original study. For instance, all SCORE projects are preregistered, and all use sample sizes that are based on a formal power analysis, whether or not these were the case in the original study. Labs may choose to include additional ‘best practices’ that may not have been present in the original study, in addition to other necessary differences between the original and replication/reproduction study. These are allowable, so long as they remain a good faith replication of the original finding. We are collecting a list of best practices which are the kinds of steps that labs are encouraged to take (and that you can recommend!) to increase the robustness and replicability/reproducibility of their work. Keep in mind that not all projects need to (or can) include all of these practices. The preregistration for this paper begins here. You can also reference this list of Frequently Asked Questions for preregistration reviewers.

Instructions for Replication/Reproduction Team General information about preregistration is available at https://cos.io/prereg. Every section should have a response from you; in the case that a section truly does not apply (e.g., “Manipulated Variables” do not exist in an observational study), you can respond with “N/A.” Some sections are indicated as multiple choice, and we ask that you bold your response(s) if one has not already been selected. All other sections are open-ended. A research team member from the Center for Open Science has provided some information for your SCORE project from the original paper and, where possible, additional feedback and materials from the original author(s). This information should not be considered a complete response to a section unless otherwise noted. The preregistration for your replication or reproduction should provide as detailed a plan as possible of what you will be doing, not just describe what was done in the original paper. That plan should be written in the future tense, and reference the original paper and any original materials or correspondence with the original author as necessary to provide context or justification of any decisions made for your protocol. You are encouraged to look over the Existing Data Reviewer Criteria that will be used to evaluate your preregistration. For each question, your response should include a complete description (state what you will do, in enough detail that others could implement your plan), any deviations (clearly state anything you added, omitted, or changed from the original study), and rationale (justify why your decisions are consistent with a good-faith replication of the original claim). After you have prepared the replication dataset, you will develop and test your analysis plan and code by using a random subset of 5% of the replication dataset (additional instructions can be found in section 21 of this preregistration template). Please upload your data cleaning and analysis scripts, as well as all other materials (e.g., codebook/data dictionary, replication dataset or dummy dataset, surveys, instructions for accessing data sources, etc.), to the OSF project associated with your replication attempt. Any files you upload for your project should be directly referenced within the document by filename so it is clear what is being used for your replication and where reviewers can find it. (For instance: “Each participant will see 16 of the 64 cat pictures. All stimuli are uploaded to the Methods & Materials component, as cat1.jpg, cat2.jpg...cat64.jpg.”) You can reach out to the SCORE Project Coordinators for additional guidance at scorecoordinator@cos.io, and you can also reference this list of Frequently Asked Questions.

Bushel claim evaluation standards This section is intended to help guide the selection of evidence and definition of inferential criteria for RR attempts based on bushel-coded claims. RR labs, reviewers, and editors are expected to consider this guidance when preparing and reviewing the preregistration. Replication teams are encouraged to read the Preregistration Review Manual for additional insight regarding reviewer and editor instructions, which includes examples of satisfactory vs unsatisfactory inferential criteria. Selecting which claims to evaluate Bushel replication, reproduction, and generalizability attempts should aim to evaluate as many claims as are possible given the data that is being used for that attempt. Generally, this should represent at least 5 focal claims, or all focal claims if there are less than 5. However, that is not to say that only 5 claims should be evaluated, or that only focal claims should be evaluated; when it comes to claim selection, the more, the merrier! The exciting benefit of evaluating bushel claims is that they provide an opportunity to interrogate the breadth of evidence available within a given paper, and we are eager to see our collaborators take full advantage of this opportunity. Reviewers and Editors are encouraged to empower the replication team to consider evaluating additional claims if it is apparent that their data contains the necessary variables to perform the analysis (or analyses) that corresponds to the statistical evidence for a given claim. Defining inferential criteria for bushel claims In Phase 1 of the SCORE program, each selected claim was limited to representing a single, significant inferential test. The statistical evidence used to support a bushel claim is not constrained to those criteria; the evidence selected from the original paper can be any form of quantitative results, including non-inferential evidence, non-significant evidence, and multiple pieces of evidence in the same claim. This expansion of the types of evidence that can be selected from the original paper introduces additional complexity to defining the inferential criteria that will be used to make the binary decision of whether or not a claim has replicated or reproduced. The programmatic goals of the SCORE program also still require the evaluation of a single, significant inferential test for each claim. To meet these competing needs, there may be two types of inferential criteria for each bushel claim: one for the “simple” test of the claim, and one for the “complex” test of the claim. The simple test of a claim The “simple” test of the claim is an evaluation that meets the programmatic requirements of SCORE, i.e., it is based on a single, significant inferential test from the original paper, which is expected to be significant in the replication attempt.
